items.question_text: A controller clears an aircraft to maintain VFR conditions and needs to specify the altitude block for the climb. Which phraseology correctly includes the altitude specification?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2 phraseology, when specifying an altitude block for a VFR climb or descent, the controller says CLIMB VFR or DESCEND VFR, followed by BETWEEN (altitude) AND (altitude) or ABOVE/BELOW (altitude). The altitude specification is appended to the climb/descend instruction, not embedded in MAINTAIN VFR CONDITIONS.

choices.text: CLIMB VFR, BETWEEN 3,000 AND 5,000.
MAINTAIN VFR CONDITIONS BETWEEN 3,000 AND 5,000.
CLIMB AND MAINTAIN VFR, ALTITUDE RESTRICTION 3,000 TO 5,000.
VFR CLIMB APPROVED, MAINTAIN BLOCK ALTITUDE 3,000 THROUGH 5,000.

items.question_text: A controller clears an aircraft to maintain VFR conditions at 4,500 feet. The controller later notices a developing overcast layer at 5,000 feet along the aircraft's route. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-2b, when in the controller's judgment there is reason to believe that flight in VFR conditions may become impractical, the controller must issue an alternative clearance that will ensure separation from all other aircraft for which the controller has separation responsibility. The prescribed phraseology is: IF UNABLE, (alternative procedure), AND ADVISE.

choices.text: Allow the aircraft to continue; the pilot is responsible for avoiding clouds and maintaining VFR.
Issue an alternative clearance ensuring separation from all aircraft for which the controller has separation responsibility.
Cancel the VFR-conditions clearance and issue an IFR clearance to the destination.
Issue a VFR-on-top clearance since the aircraft can climb above the overcast layer.

items.question_text: A controller selects a depicted VFR checkpoint as a visual holding fix for a VFR aircraft. Where are these depicted VFR checkpoints found, and why are they preferred?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4 note, VFR checkpoints are depicted on Sectional Aeronautical and Terminal Area Charts. They are preferred because they appear on charts that pilots already carry, making them easily recognizable from the air. When the pilot exhibits familiarity with the local area, other prominent geographical fixes may be used instead.

choices.text: They are published in the facility SOP and marked by ground-based visual beacons.
They are defined by GPS waypoints in the aircraft's navigation database and require RNAV capability.
They are depicted on Sectional Aeronautical and Terminal Area Charts, making them easily recognized by pilots.
They are charted on IFR en route low-altitude charts and identified by five-letter fixes.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft is holding at a visual fix and the controller needs to advise the pilot of another aircraft that is en route to the same fix from the south. Which phraseology correctly describes the traffic?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-4 phraseology, there are two traffic-description options: TRAFFIC (description) HOLDING AT (fix, altitude if known) for aircraft already at the fix, and PROCEEDING TO (fix) FROM (direction or fix) for aircraft still en route to the fix. For an aircraft not yet holding, PROCEEDING TO is the correct form.

choices.text: TRAFFIC CESSNA HOLDING AT LAKE INTERSECTION, 2,500.
TRAFFIC CESSNA ESTIMATING LAKE INTERSECTION AT TIME 35.
TRAFFIC CESSNA TRANSITIONING LAKE INTERSECTION NORTHBOUND.
TRAFFIC CESSNA PROCEEDING TO LAKE INTERSECTION FROM THE SOUTH.

items.question_text: A terminal controller and an en route controller both observe that two aircraft under pilot-applied visual separation have targets that appear likely to merge. How do the merging advisories differ between terminal and en route?
items.explanation: Per 7-2-1, the terminal merging target advisory is: 'Targets appear likely to merge.' The en route merging target advisory is: 'Radar targets appear likely to merge.' The en route version includes the word 'Radar' at the beginning; the terminal version does not.

choices.text: The en route version includes 'Radar' at the beginning; the terminal version does not.
Only the terminal version specifies the clock position of the merging traffic.
Only the en route version includes the aircraft type in the advisory.
There is no difference; both versions use identical phraseology.

items.question_text: A pilot on an IFR flight plan requests to climb through a cloud layer and then maintain VFR-on-top. The controller has a tops report of 12,000 feet. Which phraseology should the controller use?
items.explanation: Per 7-3-1b phraseology, when clearing an aircraft to climb to VFR-on-top, the controller states: CLIMB TO AND REPORT REACHING VFR-ON-TOP, followed by either TOPS REPORTED (altitude) or NO TOPS REPORTS. The pilot must report when reaching VFR-on-top conditions.

choices.text: MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP ABOVE ONE TWO THOUSAND, REPORT REACHING.
CLIMB TO AND REPORT REACHING VFR-ON-TOP, TOPS REPORTED ONE TWO THOUSAND.
CLIMB VFR CONDITIONS TO ONE TWO THOUSAND, REPORT ON TOP.
CLEARED TO CLIMB VFR-ON-TOP, CLOUD TOPS AT ONE TWO THOUSAND.

items.question_text: A controller receives a radar report indicating a VFR-on-top aircraft is at an altitude that does not match the 91.159(a) cruising altitude for its direction of flight. In which situation should the controller refrain from issuing the cruising-level advisory?
items.explanation: Per 7-3-2 note and 14 CFR 91.159(a), the VFR cruising altitude requirements exclude aircraft in a holding pattern of 2 minutes or less, and aircraft while turning. These maneuvering aircraft are exempt from the cruising altitude table, so no advisory is needed.

choices.text: The aircraft is at 4,500 feet on a magnetic course of 270 degrees in straight-and-level cruise.
The aircraft is at 7,500 feet on a magnetic course of 090 degrees at 5,000 feet AGL.
The aircraft is established in a holding pattern of 2 minutes or less.
The aircraft is cruising at 9,500 feet on a magnetic course of 360 degrees at 4,000 feet AGL.

items.question_text: A VFR-on-top aircraft is cruising at 2,500 feet MSL on a magnetic course of 045 degrees. The controller checks the VFR cruising altitude table. Does the advisory obligation apply?
items.explanation: Per 7-3-2 note, the VFR cruising altitude requirements of 14 CFR 91.159(a) apply to aircraft operating more than 3,000 feet above the surface up to and including FL180. At 2,500 feet MSL over terrain of 1,000 feet, the aircraft is only 1,500 feet AGL — well below the 3,000-foot AGL threshold where the cruising altitude rules begin.

choices.text: Yes, the controller must issue the cruising-level advisory regardless of altitude.
Yes, because VFR-on-top aircraft are subject to cruising-altitude rules at all altitudes.
No, but only because the aircraft is below 10,000 feet MSL where cruising altitude rules do not apply.
No, if the aircraft is below 3,000 feet AGL, the 91.159(a) cruising altitude rules do not apply.

items.question_text: At an airport where weather information is not available, a pilot on an IFR flight plan requests a visual approach. What does this request indicate to the controller?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-2 note, at airports where weather information is not available, a pilot request for a visual approach indicates that descent and flight to the airport can be made visually and clear of clouds. This provides the reasonable assurance needed to initiate a vector for the visual approach.

choices.text: It indicates that descent and flight to the airport can be made visually and clear of clouds, providing reasonable assurance.
It confirms that the reported ceiling is at least 500 feet above the MVA at the destination.
It authorizes the controller to cancel the aircraft's IFR flight plan.
It requires the controller to obtain a special weather observation before vectoring.

items.question_text: At an airport with an operating control tower, two aircraft are on visual approaches to the same runway. The trailing pilot reports the airport and runway in sight but does not report the preceding aircraft in sight. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-3c3, when the pilot at a towered airport reports the airport or runway in sight but NOT the preceding aircraft, the visual approach clearance may still be issued, but radar separation must be maintained until visual separation is provided. This allows the approach to proceed while ensuring separation until the pilot reports the preceding traffic.

choices.text: Deny the visual approach clearance until the pilot reports the preceding aircraft in sight.
Issue the visual approach clearance but maintain radar separation until visual separation is provided.
Cancel the visual approach and issue vectors for an instrument approach to ensure separation.
Issue the visual approach clearance without restriction since the pilot has the runway in sight.

items.question_text: At an airport without an operating control tower, what must the pilot report before ATC can clear the aircraft for a visual approach?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-3c4, at locations without an operating control tower, the pilot must report the airport in sight before a visual approach clearance can be issued. There is no preceding aircraft to follow, so only the airport-in-sight report is required. The phraseology differs from towered airports: 'CLEARED VISUAL APPROACH TO (airport name).'

choices.text: The pilot must report both the airport and any preceding aircraft in sight.
The pilot must report the runway and the ATIS code.
The pilot must report the airport in sight.
No visual report is needed; the controller initiates the clearance based on radar position.

items.question_text: A controller is conducting simultaneous visual approaches to close parallel runways. The primary radar targets of the two aircraft on adjacent final approach courses begin to touch on the radar display. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-4b2, primary radar or fusion targets on adjacent final approach courses must not touch unless visual separation is applied. If the targets are touching, the controller must either apply visual separation or take action to re-establish approved separation.

choices.text: Targets touching is acceptable as long as both aircraft are on parallel localizer courses.
The controller must cancel both visual approaches and re-vector the aircraft.
Targets may touch as long as they do not overlap and both pilots are on the tower frequency.
Primary radar targets on adjacent final approach courses must not touch unless visual separation is applied.

items.question_text: Air traffic is conducting simultaneous approaches to intersecting runways. Under what condition may staggered approaches be necessary instead of simultaneous approaches?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-4 note, although simultaneous visual approaches may be conducted to intersecting runways, staggered approaches may be necessary to meet the airport separation requirements specified in paragraph 3-10-4, Intersecting Runway/Intersecting Flight Path Separation.

choices.text: When required to meet intersecting runway separation requirements per paragraph 3-10-4.
When aircraft using the same type of approach must be sequenced along the same final course.
Staggered approaches are never authorized; only simultaneous approaches may be conducted.
When the crosswind component exceeds 15 knots on either runway.

items.question_text: When clearing an aircraft for a charted visual flight procedure to follow a preceding aircraft to the same runway, what must the pilot report before the clearance can be issued?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-5, when aircraft are following another aircraft on the CVFP, the pilot must report the preceding aircraft in sight. The charted visual landmark report alone is not sufficient when the aircraft is following another; the specific traffic must be visually acquired.

choices.text: Only the charted visual landmark identified on the CVFP chart.
The preceding aircraft in sight.
Only the airport and the runway in sight.
Both the airport in sight and the ATIS information code.

items.question_text: Before issuing an RVFP approach clearance, what altitude action must the controller take?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-6, the aircraft must be issued a descent to an altitude at or above the MVA or minimum IFR altitude before being cleared for the RVFP approach. This ensures the aircraft has terrain and obstruction clearance before transitioning to visual flight on the procedure.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft to descend at pilot's discretion to the FAF crossing altitude.
Maintain the last assigned IFR altitude until the aircraft reports established on the RVFP.
Issue a descent to an altitude at or above the MVA or minimum IFR altitude.
Issue a descent to pattern altitude and clear for the approach.

items.question_text: A controller clears an aircraft for a contact approach, but weather conditions are marginal. What must the controller include in the clearance to address the possibility that the contact approach cannot be completed?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-7e, when weather conditions are such that a contact approach may not be practical, an alternative clearance must be issued. The phraseology appends: IF NOT POSSIBLE, (alternative procedures), AND ADVISE. This gives the pilot a defined backup procedure if conditions prevent completing the contact approach.

choices.text: No alternative is required; the pilot is responsible for determining whether the approach is feasible.
Require the pilot to report the airport in sight every 60 seconds during the approach.
Issue a simultaneous instrument approach clearance as a backup to the contact approach.
Include an alternative clearance using the phrase: IF NOT POSSIBLE, (alternative procedures), AND ADVISE.

items.question_text: At an airport with an operating control tower, the reported ceiling is 800 feet broken. A VFR aircraft requests to depart and operate within the surface area without requesting SVFR. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-1b, VFR operations may not be authorized beneath a broken or overcast ceiling within a surface area when the reported ceiling at the primary airport is less than 1,000 feet. A Special VFR clearance is required. Note: VFR operations ABOVE such a ceiling remain authorized under 14 CFR 91.155(a).

choices.text: Deny standard VFR beneath the ceiling and inform the pilot that a Special VFR clearance is required.
Authorize the VFR operation if the pilot can maintain the required cloud clearance of 500 feet below the ceiling.
Authorize the VFR operation using basic VFR minima; the pilot is responsible for staying clear of clouds.
Authorize the operation only if the aircraft is equipped with an operable transponder and Mode C.

items.question_text: A pilot requests a Special VFR clearance to operate in Class G airspace when the visibility is below basic VFR minima. Is this clearance available?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-1a, SVFR operations are authorized only in the surface areas of Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E airspace. SVFR is not authorized in Class G airspace (no surface-area control exists) or in Class A airspace (exclusively IFR).

choices.text: Yes, SVFR is available in all classes of airspace below 10,000 feet MSL.
No, SVFR operations are authorized only in Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E surface areas.
Yes, SVFR is a pilot authority under 14 CFR 91.157 and does not require controller action.
No, SVFR is only available at airports with a published instrument approach procedure.

items.question_text: Why is radar separation normally not authorized between SVFR fixed-wing aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-3 note 2, SVFR fixed-wing aircraft must maintain 1-mile flight visibility and remain clear of clouds. These requirements can necessitate unpredicted maneuvering to avoid clouds, which makes radar separation unreliable. Nonradar or visual separation must be used instead. Radar separation may, however, be applied for SVFR helicopters when the facility has certified tower radar displays.

choices.text: SVFR fixed-wing aircraft operate only in areas below radar coverage, making radar separation technically impossible.
Radar separation is reserved exclusively for IFR aircraft and may never be applied to SVFR operations.
SVFR fixed-wing aircraft must maintain 1-mile flight visibility and remain clear of clouds, which can necessitate unpredicted maneuvering that makes radar separation unreliable.
SVFR fixed-wing aircraft are not equipped with transponders, so radar identification is unavailable.

items.question_text: An FSS has been authorized to transmit SVFR clearances for local operations. Under what condition may more than one SVFR aircraft operate in the surface area at the same time under this FSS authorization?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-5b, control facilities may authorize an FSS to transmit SVFR clearances so that normally only one aircraft at a time operates in the surface area. The exception: more than one aircraft may operate if the pilots agree that they will maintain visual separation from each other.

choices.text: When the FSS has radar equipment capable of providing separation between the aircraft.
When each aircraft remains on a discrete frequency assigned by the FSS specialist.
Never; an FSS may never authorize more than one SVFR aircraft in the surface area simultaneously.
When the pilots agree that they will maintain visual separation from each other.

items.question_text: A controller authorizes an aircraft to climb to VFR and needs to specify the area within which the pilot must maintain SVFR conditions until reaching VFR. Which phraseology correctly specifies this area?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-6 phraseology, the controller states: CLIMB TO V-F-R WITHIN (name) SURFACE AREA / WITHIN (a specified distance) MILES FROM (airport name) AIRPORT, MAINTAIN SPECIAL V-F-R CONDITIONS UNTIL REACHING V-F-R. Two options exist for specifying the area: by surface area name or by distance from the airport.

choices.text: CLIMB TO V-F-R WITHIN LINCOLN SURFACE AREA, MAINTAIN SPECIAL V-F-R CONDITIONS UNTIL REACHING V-F-R.
CLIMB VFR WITHIN 5 MILES OF THE AIRPORT, REPORT REACHING VFR CONDITIONS.
CLEARED TO CLIMB VFR, REMAIN CLEAR OF CLOUDS UNTIL VFR-ON-TOP.
MAINTAIN SVFR CONDITIONS WITHIN THE CONTROL ZONE UNTIL ADVISED.

items.question_text: Ground visibility at an airport is officially reported as 3/4 mile. A Part 121 domestic scheduled air carrier is ready to depart under IFR. Does the aircraft need an SVFR clearance to depart?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-7 note, 14 CFR Part 121 permits landing or takeoff by domestic scheduled air carriers where a local surface restriction to visibility is specified in the carrier's operations specifications (OpSpecs). The SVFR requirement for ground visibility below 1 mile does not apply to Part 121 carriers operating under their OpSpecs authorization.

choices.text: Yes, all aircraft need an SVFR clearance whenever ground visibility is below 1 mile.
No SVFR clearance is needed if the carrier's OpSpecs authorize operations at that visibility.
The aircraft may depart under IFR without SVFR, but only if it uses a published instrument departure.
Yes, unless the aircraft is a wide-body jet equipped with autoland capability.

items.question_text: A terminal facility has established procedures for providing basic radar services to VFR aircraft. To which types of airspace must these services be applied?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-1b, basic radar services for VFR aircraft must be applied to Class B, Class C, TRSA, and Class D surface areas where procedures have been established for this purpose. These four airspace/surface types form the domain of the basic radar service program.

choices.text: Only Class B and Class C airspace where separation services are mandatory.
All controlled airspace below FL180 where radar coverage exists.
Class B, Class C, TRSA, and Class D surface areas where procedures have been established.
Only the outer area of Class C and the TRSA service area.

items.question_text: A departing VFR aircraft wants traffic advisories after takeoff. According to the note in 7-6-10, what is expected of the pilot before departure?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-10 note, departing aircraft desiring traffic information are expected to request the service and to state their proposed direction of flight upon initial contact with ground control. This allows the controller to provide the correct departure control frequency and prepare appropriate traffic information after the aircraft is radar identified.

choices.text: File a VFR flight plan with FSS and advise ground control of the filed route.
Contact departure control directly after takeoff and request advisories at that time.
State only the destination airport; the controller will determine the direction of flight from the filed plan.
Request radar traffic advisories and state the proposed direction of flight upon initial contact with ground control.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft is receiving basic radar services when the controller's frequency becomes saturated with IFR arrivals. What principle governs whether radar service must continue?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-11, basic radar services should be provided to the extent possible, workload permitting. This is a qualified obligation — when workload precludes continued service, the controller may terminate radar service to the VFR aircraft. This is different from mandatory separation services which cannot be workload-dependent.

choices.text: Basic radar services should be provided to the extent possible, workload permitting; the controller may terminate when workload precludes continued service.
Basic radar services are mandatory and cannot be terminated for any reason once established.
All VFR services must be immediately terminated whenever IFR workload increases.
The controller must transfer the VFR aircraft to another frequency capable of providing continued radar service.

items.question_text: During hours when the tower is not in operation, a VFR aircraft requests landing information. The controller attempts to obtain wind information from the FSS but the FSS reports it does not have the current wind. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-12 note, wind information is provided from the FSS or WSO when the tower is inoperative. If that information is not available from those sources, the controller must inform the pilot that wind information is not available. The controller should not fabricate or use stale wind data.

choices.text: Provide the last recorded wind reading from before the tower closed.
Inform the pilot that wind information is not available.
Tune the ATIS frequency and relay the automated weather observation.
Ask the pilot for a wind report and use that as the official wind information.

items.question_text: An aircraft requests basic radar service but the primary approach control frequency is fully occupied handling an arrival push. What must the controller do if the pilot still desires the service?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-2b, if an aircraft desires basic radar service but the primary frequency is congested, the controller should provide the service to the extent possible using an available frequency. This could involve using a secondary frequency or coordinating service through another control position.

choices.text: Deny service and instruct the aircraft to try again when the frequency becomes less congested.
Hand off the aircraft to the overlying ARTCC for radar service provision.
Provide the service to the extent possible using an available frequency.
Accept the request but place the aircraft in a holding pattern until the frequency clears.

items.question_text: A controller is sequencing VFR arrivals to close parallel runways that are 2,000 feet apart. A faster trailing aircraft on the left runway is closing on a slower lead aircraft on the right runway. What restriction applies?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-7c, when parallel runways are less than 2,500 feet apart, an aircraft must not overtake another aircraft established on the adjacent final approach course. This restriction prevents side-by-side operations at close lateral proximity where wake turbulence or course deviations could compromise safety.

choices.text: The trailing aircraft may overtake if it maintains 1,000 feet vertical separation above the lead aircraft.
Overtaking is permitted when both aircraft are on visual approaches and both pilots report each other in sight.
No restriction applies; aircraft on separate runways may pass each other freely regardless of runway spacing.
The trailing aircraft must not overtake the aircraft on the adjacent final approach course.

items.question_text: A controller assigned a VFR aircraft in a TRSA an altitude of 4,500 feet on a magnetic course of 270 degrees for separation purposes. The separation requirement no longer exists. The assigned altitude is contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 for that direction of flight. What phraseology should the controller use?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-5c, when an altitude assignment that is contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 is no longer needed or the aircraft exits the TRSA, the controller must advise the aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes. The prescribed phraseology is: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.

choices.text: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.
RESUME OWN NAVIGATION AND VFR ALTITUDES AT YOUR DISCRETION.
ALTITUDE RESTRICTION CANCELLED, FLY VFR ALTITUDES.
DISREGARD ASSIGNED ALTITUDE, MAINTAIN VFR.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft departs a controlled airport within a TRSA and is receiving TRSA services. The aircraft is now 2 miles from the TRSA boundary. The controller would like to terminate TRSA service. What must the controller continue providing until the aircraft actually leaves the TRSA?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-7c, the controller must provide separation until the aircraft leaves the TRSA. TRSA departure information (frequency, beacon code) and separation services continue through the TRSA boundary. The leaving phraseology may then be issued at the boundary.

choices.text: Traffic advisories only; separation responsibility transfers to the pilot at the TRSA boundary.
Separation until the aircraft leaves the TRSA.
Radar identification until the aircraft reaches 40 NM from the departure airport.
Beacon code assignment only; separation ends when the aircraft reaches cruising altitude.

items.question_text: An aircraft without an operable transponder needs to operate within Class C airspace for an airshow. Under what condition may this be authorized despite the normal equipment requirements?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-1, while two-way radio communications and an operational transponder are normally required for Class C operations, operations without this equipment may be conducted when authorized by Letter of Agreement (LOA), facility directive, or special authorization. This provides operational flexibility for special events and circumstances.

choices.text: Never; the equipment requirements for Class C airspace are absolute and cannot be waived.
Only if the aircraft is accompanied by an FAA-certified chase plane with the required equipment.
By Letter of Agreement, facility directive, or special authorization.
Only during daylight VFR conditions when the pilot can maintain visual separation.

items.question_text: A Class C facility has excellent radar coverage over most of its service area but poor coverage in one quadrant due to terrain. May the controller deny Class C radar service to aircraft in the well-covered portions based on the limited coverage in one area?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-2 note, limited radar coverage in one portion of a Class C area does not justify denial of Class C radar service in the entire area. Service must continue to be provided in portions of the area where adequate radar coverage exists.

choices.text: Yes, limited coverage in any portion permits denial of service throughout the entire Class C area.
Yes, but the controller must issue a NOTAM advising pilots of the service suspension.
No, but service may be reduced to basic radar services only in all portions of the Class C area.
No, limited coverage in one portion does not justify denial of radar service in the entire area.

items.question_text: A hot air balloon is operating in Class C airspace near the final approach path. An IFR arrival is on a 5-mile final. What separation must the controller provide between the balloon and the IFR aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-6b, hot air balloons need not be separated from IFR aircraft. The controller must still issue traffic information and safety alerts as appropriate. The balloon exception exists because balloons have limited maneuverability and cannot maintain standard separation from faster IFR traffic.

choices.text: No separation is required, but traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.
Standard IFR radar separation of 3 miles must be applied.
500 feet vertical separation must be maintained at all times.
Visual separation must be applied; the IFR pilot must report the balloon in sight.

items.question_text: An aircraft departs an uncontrolled airport located under Class C airspace. There is no LOA between the airport and the Class C facility. How should the controller handle this aircraft when it calls for Class C service after departure?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-7c, aircraft departing uncontrolled airports within Class C airspace must be handled using procedures advertised in the Chart Supplement (formerly Airport/Facility Directory). These published procedures provide the method for integrating uncontrolled-field departures into Class C traffic when no formal LOA exists.

choices.text: Deny Class C service and instruct the aircraft to remain clear of Class C airspace.
Handle the aircraft using procedures advertised in the Chart Supplement for that airport.
Provide only basic radar service since the departure airport is uncontrolled.
Treat the aircraft as a VFR pop-up and sequence it behind all primary-airport departures.

items.question_text: What specific equipment must an aircraft have to operate within Class B airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-1b, Class B operations require both: (1) an operable two-way radio capable of communications with ATC on appropriate frequencies for that Class B airspace, AND (2) an operable radar beacon transponder with automatic altitude reporting capability (Mode C) and ADS-B Out equipment. Both items are required, not optional.

choices.text: An operable two-way radio; transponder equipment is recommended but not required for VFR aircraft.
A two-way radio OR a transponder with altitude reporting; at least one of the two.
An operable two-way radio AND an operable radar beacon transponder with automatic altitude reporting capability and ADS-B Out equipment.
A two-way radio, a basic transponder, and an operable DME receiver for navigation within the airspace.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft in Class B airspace is assigned a heading of 360 degrees and altitude of 3,000 feet. The pilot realizes that maintaining this altitude at the assigned location would require flying through clouds, violating VFR cloud-clearance requirements. What is the pilot expected to do?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-2 note 1, a pilot operating in accordance with VFR is expected to advise ATC if compliance with assigned headings, routes, or altitudes will cause violation of any part of the CFR. Flying through clouds would violate VFR cloud-clearance requirements, so the pilot must inform ATC and request an amended clearance.

choices.text: Comply with the ATC clearance without question; ATC instructions override all VFR requirements.
Cancel VFR and request an IFR clearance to operate at the assigned altitude in the clouds.
Deviate from the clearance as needed to maintain VFR and advise ATC after the deviation.
Advise ATC that compliance will cause violation of the CFR and request an amended clearance.

items.question_text: A controller vectors a VFR aircraft on a heading that will take it outside Class B airspace temporarily before it re-enters. What must the controller communicate to the aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-3b, aircraft should be vectored to remain in Class B airspace after entry unless operational advantage is lost. If the aircraft will leave and re-enter Class B airspace, the controller must inform the aircraft when it is leaving and when it is re-entering Class B airspace. This is critical because outside Class B, the VFR aircraft loses the protection of Class B separation services.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft when it is leaving and re-entering Class B airspace.
No notification is needed; the pilot is responsible for monitoring airspace boundaries.
Issue a new Class B clearance number each time the aircraft re-enters the airspace.
Hand off the aircraft to the overlying ARTCC until it returns to Class B airspace.

items.question_text: At an airport where procedures have been established for arriving VFR aircraft to contact approach control for landing information, a VFR arrival makes initial contact with the tower on the local control frequency. The controller wants the aircraft to receive landing and traffic information from approach control first. What should the controller instruct the aircraft to do?

choices.text: Contact approach control for landing and traffic information upon this initial contact with the tower.
Remain on tower frequency until the tower provides the landing sequence number.
Squawk standby and await a call from approach control on the guard frequency.
Proceed to the ATIS frequency and obtain the current weather broadcast before contacting approach.

items.explanation: Per 7-1-3d, the aircraft may be instructed to contact approach control for landing and traffic information upon initial contact with the tower. This procedure allows approach control to issue the four required items (wind, runway, altimeter; traffic information; time/place to contact tower; and the tower contact instruction) before the tower assumes control of the arrival.

items.instruction: Which transmission should the controller issue?
items.question_text: A controller clears an aircraft to climb to VFR-on-top through an overcast layer whose tops are unknown. The controller wants the aircraft to level at 8,000 feet and advise if still not on top at that altitude so an alternative clearance can be issued. The aircraft is currently at 4,000 feet IFR.

choices.text: CLIMB TO AND REPORT REACHING VFR-ON-TOP, NO TOPS REPORTS. IF NOT ON TOP AT 8,000, MAINTAIN 8,000, AND ADVISE.
CLIMB TO 8,000 AND REPORT VFR-ON-TOP. MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP ABOVE 8,000.
MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP UNTIL 8,000. ADVISE IF UNABLE TO COMPLY.
CLEARED VFR-ON-TOP. IF NOT ON TOP BY 8,000 REVERT TO IFR AND CONTACT APPROACH.

items.explanation: Per 7-3-1b phraseology, when no tops report is available, the controller says NO TOPS REPORTS. The fallback safety provision is: IF NOT ON TOP AT (altitude), MAINTAIN (altitude), AND ADVISE. This prevents the aircraft from climbing indefinitely without reaching VFR conditions while giving the controller a defined point at which to reassess the clearance.

items.instruction: What procedure applies to the go-around?
items.question_text: An aircraft on an IFR flight plan executes a go-around from a visual approach at an airport without an operating control tower. The pilot has the airport environment in sight but cannot complete the landing due to a deer on the runway. What is expected of the aircraft after the go-around?

choices.text: The aircraft is expected to proceed in accordance with visual flight rules while ATC provides appropriate IFR separation until the aircraft lands or cancels IFR.
The aircraft must immediately fly the published missed approach procedure for the RNAV approach to the same runway.
The aircraft must cancel IFR and operate under VFR for the remainder of the flight.
The aircraft must hold over the airport at the MVA and await further ATC clearance.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-1b, at airports without an operating control tower, aircraft executing a go-around after a visual approach are expected to proceed in accordance with visual flight rules. There is no published missed approach segment for a visual approach because it is not a standard instrument approach procedure — a go-around must be handled as any go-around with appropriate IFR separation provided.

items.instruction: What clearance should the controller issue?
items.question_text: A pilot on an IFR flight plan requests a contact approach to an airport where the reported ground visibility is 1 mile but the controller is concerned that fog may reduce visibility below 1 mile before the aircraft reaches the airport. The pilot will approach from the south and the ILS Runway 18 provides a reliable backup.

choices.text: CLEARED CONTACT APPROACH. IF NOT POSSIBLE, CLIMB TO 3,000 AND FLY HEADING 270 FOR VECTORS TO THE ILS RUNWAY 18 APPROACH, AND ADVISE.
CONTACT APPROACH NOT AUTHORIZED. MAINTAIN 3,000 AND EXPECT VECTORS TO THE ILS RUNWAY 18 APPROACH.
CLEARED CONTACT APPROACH. MAINTAIN 3,000 UNTIL THE AIRPORT IS IN SIGHT, THEN PROCEED DIRECT AND ADVISE.
CLEARED CONTACT APPROACH. IF NOT POSSIBLE, CANCEL IFR, PROCEED VFR TO THE AIRPORT, AND ADVISE.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-7e, when weather conditions are such that a contact approach may not be practical, the controller must issue an alternative clearance. The prescribed phraseology is: CLEARED CONTACT APPROACH, (and if required, AT OR BELOW altitude routing), IF NOT POSSIBLE, (alternative procedures), AND ADVISE.

items.instruction: How should the controller handle this situation?
items.question_text: An SVFR aircraft has been cleared to enter the Class D surface area and is approximately 2 minutes from landing. An IFR aircraft then calls ready for departure on the same runway. Issuing an immediate takeoff clearance to the IFR aircraft would require the SVFR aircraft to be sent around or held outside the surface area. What may the controller do?

choices.text: Immediately cancel the SVFR clearance; IFR always takes absolute priority regardless of operational efficiency.
Permit the SVFR aircraft to complete its landing because less overall delay results from letting it finish than from holding or sending it around.
Direct the SVFR aircraft to exit the surface area immediately and re-enter after the IFR departure.
Clear the IFR aircraft for immediate takeoff since SVFR aircraft have no priority under any circumstances.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-2 note, the priority afforded IFR aircraft over SVFR aircraft is not intended to be so rigidly applied that inefficient use of airspace results. The controller has the prerogative of permitting completion of an SVFR operation already in progress when an IFR aircraft becomes a factor, if better overall efficiency results. The example in the paragraph illustrates exactly this scenario — letting the SVFR aircraft land may cause less overall delay.

items.front: Which facilities may use the noise-abatement condition to issue a VFR-conditions clearance?
items.back: TERMINAL facilities only. The noise-abatement condition in 7-1-2a2 is restricted to terminal facilities and is not available to en route controllers.

items.front: When must a controller issue an alternative clearance to an aircraft maintaining VFR conditions?
items.back: When, in the controller's judgment, there is reason to believe that flight in VFR conditions may become impractical. Issue: IF UNABLE, (alternative procedure), AND ADVISE.

items.front: When a pilot says 'have numbers,' does this confirm receipt of the ATIS broadcast?
items.back: No. Per 7-1-3 note, pilot use of 'have numbers' does NOT indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast. It only means the pilot already has wind, runway, and altimeter information. The ATIS code must still be verified separately.

items.front: When are depicted VFR checkpoints NOT required for visual holding of VFR aircraft?
items.back: When the pilot exhibits familiarity with the local area. In that case, any prominent, easily recognized geographical fix may be used for visual holding.

items.front: When a nonapproach control tower accepts visual separation authorization for arrivals, who retains responsibility for IFR separation before and after?
items.back: The Approach/Departure/En Route facility. The tower becomes responsible for the visual separation itself, but IFR separation before and after application of visual separation remains an IFR control function of the radar facility.

items.front: What must be assured before visual separation may be applied between aircraft?
items.back: Other approved separation must be assured both BEFORE and AFTER the application of visual separation. To ensure this, consider: aircraft performance, wake turbulence, closure rate, routes of flight, known weather conditions, and aircraft positions.

items.front: When is VFR-on-top prohibited as a method to separate holding aircraft from each other or from en route aircraft?
items.back: Between sunset and sunrise. Per 7-3-1c, do not clear an aircraft to maintain VFR-on-top between sunset and sunrise to separate holding aircraft from each other or from en route aircraft.

items.front: What ATC services must continue for an aircraft cleared VFR-on-top even though IFR separation is suspended?
items.back: Traffic advisories, safety alerts, and merging target procedures. Per 7-3-1 note 2, these services must continue being provided to VFR-on-top aircraft even though IFR separation is suspended.

items.front: What VFR-on-top cruising altitudes apply for magnetic courses 0 through 179 degrees above 3,000 feet AGL up to FL180?
items.back: ODD cardinal altitudes plus 500 feet: e.g., 3,500, 5,500, 7,500, 9,500, etc.

items.front: What phraseology does a controller use to inform a VFR-on-top aircraft of the correct cruising levels for its direction of flight?
items.back: VFR-ON-TOP CRUISING LEVELS FOR YOUR DIRECTION OF FLIGHT ARE: (more than 3,000 feet above the surface to FL 180): ODD/EVEN ALTITUDES/FLIGHT LEVELS PLUS FIVE HUNDRED FEET.

items.front: Does a visual approach have a published missed approach segment?
items.back: No. A visual approach is not a standard instrument approach procedure and has no missed approach segment. A go-around must be handled as any go-around — appropriate IFR separation must be provided until the aircraft lands or the pilot cancels IFR.

items.front: Who is responsible for terrain and obstruction avoidance during a go-around after a visual approach?
items.back: The pilot. Per 7-4-1 note, the pilot is responsible for their own terrain and obstruction avoidance during a go-around after conducting a visual approach. The facility can assign headings towards the lowest terrain and obstructions.

items.front: What are the minimum reported weather conditions required to initiate vectors for a visual approach at an airport with weather reporting?
items.back: Reported ceiling at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA and visibility 3 miles or greater. At airports without weather reporting, there must be reasonable assurance (area weather reports, PIREPs, or pilot request) that descent can be made visually and clear of clouds.

items.front: Which facilities may clear an aircraft for a visual approach?
items.back: ARTCCs and approach controls. Towers may also exercise this authority when authorized by a Letter of Agreement (LOA) with the facility providing IFR service, or by a facility directive at collocated facilities.

items.front: What must all aircraft be informed of when approaches are being conducted to parallel, intersecting, or converging runways?
items.back: All aircraft must be informed that approaches are being conducted to parallel, intersecting, or converging runways. This notification may be accomplished through use of the ATIS broadcast.

items.front: What are the conditions that must be met before clearing an aircraft for a Charted Visual Flight Procedure (CVFP)?
items.back: 1. Operating control tower. 2. Published name of CVFP and landing runway specified in the clearance. 3. Reported ceiling ≥ 500 ft above MVA/MIA and visibility ≥ 3 miles. 4. Visual reference: for aircraft not following another — pilot reports airport or charted visual landmark in sight; for aircraft following another — pilot reports preceding aircraft in sight.

items.front: Who is authorized to use RNAV Visual Flight Procedures (RVFPs)?
items.back: Authorized operators only, as specified in the facility RVFP Letter of Agreement (LOA). RVFPs are special procedures flown in VMC and clear of clouds, available only to operators approved through the LOA process.

items.front: What is the pilot responsible for during a contact approach?
items.back: Maintaining required flight visibility, cloud clearance, and terrain/obstruction clearance. Unless otherwise restricted, the pilot may find it necessary to descend, climb, and/or fly a circuitous route to the airport to maintain cloud clearance.

items.front: In which airspace classes may Special VFR operations be authorized?
items.back: Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class E surface areas. SVFR is not authorized in Class A airspace or Class G airspace.

items.front: When is a Special VFR clearance required for operations beneath a ceiling?
items.back: When the reported ceiling at the primary airport is less than 1,000 feet and the operation is beneath a broken or overcast ceiling within the surface area. Standard VFR beneath the ceiling is not authorized; an SVFR clearance is required. (VFR operations ABOVE the ceiling are authorized under 14 CFR 91.155(a).)

items.front: What is the priority relationship between SVFR and IFR aircraft, and what flexibility does the controller have?
items.back: SVFR flights may be approved only if arriving and departing IFR aircraft are not delayed. However, the rule is not rigid — a controller may permit an SVFR operation already in progress to complete if better overall efficiency results, even if an IFR aircraft becomes a factor.

items.front: What separation methods are available for SVFR helicopters and IFR aircraft, and how does this differ from SVFR fixed-wing?
items.back: SVFR helicopters: nonradar, visual, or IFR radar separation. SVFR fixed-wing: only nonradar or visual separation. Radar separation is not authorized for SVFR fixed-wing operations because of the 1-mile flight visibility and cloud-clearance requirements that can result in unpredicted maneuvering.

items.front: What is the key condition for authorizing local SVFR operations for a specified period?
items.back: The aircraft can be recalled when traffic or weather conditions require. Upon request, local SVFR operations may be authorized for a specified period such as a series of landings and takeoffs.

items.front: May a helicopter conduct SVFR flight when ground visibility is below 1 mile?
items.back: Yes. 14 CFR Part 91 does not prohibit helicopter SVFR flight when visibility is less than 1 mile. The visibility restriction below 1 mile for SVFR applies only to fixed-wing aircraft.

items.front: At what approximate distance from the runway does the transfer of communications from approach to tower ordinarily occur for VFR arrivals?
items.back: At least 5 miles from the runway. The exact point is determined by prior coordination between the tower and approach control and varies depending on the runway in use, weather, and other factors.

items.front: When is target resolution not authorized as a separation method in TRSA operations?
items.back: When ISR (Inhibited Steered Radar) is being displayed. Target resolution may be used as a separation method between participating VFR aircraft and IFR or other participating VFR aircraft, except when ISR is being displayed.

items.front: What is assumed about departing VFR aircraft at controlled airports within a TRSA?
items.back: They are assumed to want TRSA service unless the pilot states 'negative TRSA service' or makes a similar comment. Pilots are expected to inform the controller of intended destination, route of flight, and altitude.

items.front: What phraseology is used when a VFR aircraft leaves the TRSA?
items.back: LEAVING THE (name) TRSA, and as appropriate: RESUME OWN NAVIGATION, REMAIN THIS FREQUENCY FOR TRAFFIC ADVISORIES, RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED, SQUAWK ONE TWO ZERO ZERO.

items.front: What does 14 CFR 91.131 require of large turbine engine-powered airplanes operating to or from a primary Class B airport?
items.back: Unless otherwise authorized by ATC, they must operate at or above the designated floors of the Class B airspace area while within the lateral limits of the Class B airspace.
